disease.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)
disease.note: Chronic, slowly progressive cholestatic liver disease characterized by immune-mediated destruction of small intrahepatic bile ducts. Strongly associated with anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA). Previously called primary biliary cirrhosis (renamed as most patients do not have cirrhosis at diagnosis).

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Decompensated PBC -> EMERGENCY; Incomplete UDCA response or cirrhosis -> URGENT second-line therapy; Asymptomatic/good response -> OUTPATIENT follow-up

historyFeatures.note: Often worst at night, palms/soles, can be debilitating
Fat malabsorption from bile salt deficiency

examFeatures.note: Ascites, spider angiomas, caput medusae when cirrhotic

subtypes.name: PBC (Asymptomatic)
subtypes.note: Increasingly detected through abnormal LFTs (elevated ALP, GGT) in otherwise asymptomatic individuals. Positive AMA. Normal bilirubin. Many will eventually develop symptoms.

subtypes.name: PBC (Pruritus-predominant)
subtypes.note: Debilitating pruritus as main symptom. Can be present without jaundice. Associated with higher bilirubin levels, worse prognosis. Responds to bile acid sequestrants, rifampin, or IBAT inhibitors.

subtypes.name: PBC with Cirrhosis/Decompensation
subtypes.note: End-stage PBC. Jaundice, portal hypertension, ascites, encephalopathy. High Mayo Risk Score. Transplant evaluation indicated. Hepatocellular carcinoma risk lower than other cirrhosis etiologies but still present.

subtypes.name: PBC-AIH Overlap Syndrome
subtypes.note: Features of both PBC (AMA+, cholestatic LFTs, florid bile duct lesions) and AIH (ANA/ASMA+, interface hepatitis, elevated IgG). Requires combined UDCA + immunosuppression.

laboratory.name: ALP (alkaline phosphatase)
laboratory.indication: hallmark cholestatic enzyme; elevated in PBC; used for treatment response (target ALP <1.67x ULN or normalization)

laboratory.name: GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase)
laboratory.indication: elevated in cholestasis; helps confirm hepatic origin of ALP

laboratory.name: ALT and AST
laboratory.indication: mildly elevated (2-4x ULN); if >5x ULN, consider AIH overlap

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated in advanced disease; strong prognostic marker; part of Mayo score

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: prolonged due to vitamin K malabsorption or synthetic failure

laboratory.name: Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) by IF or ELISA
laboratory.indication: positive in 95% of PBC; M2 subtype (anti-PDC-E2) is diagnostic

laboratory.name: Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA)
laboratory.indication: positive in 50%; anti-sp100 and anti-gp210 are PBC-specific ANA patterns

laboratory.name: Serum IgM
laboratory.indication: elevated in PBC (in contrast to IgG in AIH); >1x ULN supports diagnosis

laboratory.name: Lipid profile (total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, triglycerides)
laboratory.indication: significant hyperlipidemia common in PBC (lipoprotein X); paradoxically low cardiovascular risk compared to general population

laboratory.name: Vitamin A, D, E, K levels
laboratory.indication: fat-soluble vitamin deficiency from chronic cholestasis

laboratory.name: Mayo Risk Score (bilirubin, albumin, age, prothrombin time, edema score)
laboratory.indication: validated prognostic model for PBC survival

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: exclude biliary obstruction; assess for cirrhosis, portal hypertension; HCC surveillance if cirrhotic

imaging.name: MRCP/MRI cholangiography
imaging.indication: if atypical presentation (AMA-, male, cholestasis) to rule out PSC; PBC shows normal large bile ducts

imaging.name: Transient elastography (FibroScan)
imaging.indication: liver stiffness measurement predicts fibrosis stage and clinical outcomes; >9.6 kPa suggests advanced fibrosis

imaging.name: Liver biopsy
imaging.indication: not required for diagnosis if AMA+ and cholestatic LFTs. Indicated if: AMA-negative PBC suspected, AIH overlap suspected, or staging needed. Histology: florid bile duct lesions, non-suppurative destructive cholangitis, granulomas (stage 1), ductular proliferation (stage 2), fibrosis (stage 3), cirrhosis (stage 4).

management.protocolReference: AASLD Practice Guidelines: Primary Biliary Cholangitis; EASL Clinical Practice Guidelines: Primary Biliary Cholangitis

pathways.criteria: AMA+, elevated ALP, no cirrhosis, good biochemical response anticipated
pathways.treatment: First-line: ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 13-15mg/kg/day PO in divided doses (e.g., 500mg BID for 70kg). Monitor ALP, bilirubin, ALT at 6 and 12 months. Adequate biochemical response at 1 year (various criteria): Paris-I (ALP ≤1.5x ULN, AST ≤1.5x ULN, bilirubin ≤1mg/dL) or Barcelona (ALP decrease >40% or normalization). If adequate: continue UDCA lifelong, monitor q6m with LFTs and FibroScan q1-2 years.

pathways.criteria: After 12 months UDCA, ALP >1.67x ULN and/or bilirubin >1mg/dL (Paris-II or Toronto criteria)
pathways.treatment: Add second-line therapy: obeticholic acid (OCA) 5mg PO daily, titrate to 10mg PO daily if tolerated after 3-6 months (monitor for pruritus worsening; 45% experience pruritus). Alternative: fibrates (fenofibrate 145-200mg PO daily or bezafibrate 400mg PO daily) — improve ALP and may reduce pruritus (monitor liver function, creatinine, myopathy). Budesonide 9mg PO daily (non-cirrhotic only) if inflammatory activity prominent. Continue UDCA. Repeat liver stiffness assessment. Transplant evaluation if progresses to cirrhosis.

Continue UDCA. Add OCA with caution (avoid in Child-Pugh B/C; 5mg once weekly in CTP B). HCC surveillance US + AFP q6m (risk lower than other cirrhosis but not zero). Variceal screening EGD. Screen for osteoporosis (DXA scan q2 years). Supplement fat-soluble vitamins if deficient (ADEK). Monitor for pruritus, fatigue. Consider transplant evaluation if Child-Pugh class B develops.

pathways.criteria: Child-Pugh B/C, jaundice (bilirubin >2mg/dL), ascites, encephalopathy, variceal bleeding
pathways.treatment: Transplant evaluation and listing. Manage complications (diuretics, lactulose, variceal banding). Continue UDCA (may improve labs but does not change need for transplant). OCA is CONTRAINDICATED in decompensated cirrhosis. Manage pruritus symptomatically. Mayo Risk Score predicts survival without transplant. MELD-based allocation prioritization.

pathways.criteria: Clinically significant pruritus (scoring >5 on 0-10 scale), inadequate response to UDCA alone
pathways.treatment: Step therapy: (1) Cholestyramine 4g PO pre/post meals (separate UDCA by 4h). (2) Rifampin 150-300mg PO BID (monitor LFTs, risk of DILI). (3) Naltrexone 12.5-50mg PO daily (opioid antagonist; start 12.5mg to avoid withdrawal). (4) Sertraline 75-100mg PO daily. (5) IBAT inhibitor (maralixibat or odevixibat) if refractory. (6) UVB phototherapy, nasobiliary drainage, plasmapheresis for intractable cases. Liver transplant for pruritus alone (rare indication).

disease.emergencyPriority: low for asymptomatic; medium for symptomatic; high for decompensated
